questions.question: Why are large job schedulers vulnerable at common wall-clock boundaries like midnight?
options: Many jobs align naturally and can create synchronized dispatch spikes.
Because schedulers cannot store timestamps precisely.
Because midnight disables retry logic in most systems.
Because shard ownership only works in daylight hours.

questions.question: What question about missed runs most changes a scheduler design?
options: Whether missed jobs should be replayed, skipped, or coalesced after downtime.
Whether operators prefer tabs or spaces in the UI.
Whether jobs use JSON or YAML comments.
Whether the scheduler logo is company-specific.
questions.explanation: Outage catch-up semantics shape both control plane and safety.

questions.question: Which metric best shows that a scheduler is falling behind?
options: Dispatch lag and overdue-job count.
Average job-description length.
The number of schedulers in the documentation set.
Median worker hostname length.
questions.explanation: Lag reveals when jobs are due but not dispatched.

questions.question: Why is jitter often a core feature rather than an optional optimization?
options: It spreads naturally aligned workloads and reduces synchronized load spikes.
It guarantees exactly-once execution.
It removes the need for shard leases.
It makes all retries succeed faster.
questions.explanation: Schedulers need to smooth concentrated demand.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with letting the scheduler own retry policy centrally?
options: You gain visibility and safety controls but add platform complexity and some product rigidity.
You eliminate the need for idempotent jobs.
You make all downstream incidents harmless.
You prevent deadlines from expiring.
questions.explanation: Central policy is powerful but not free.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly mitigated by shard leases?
options: A scheduler node dying after claiming responsibility for due jobs.
A worker returning an application error.
A monitoring dashboard rendering slowly.
A queue retaining messages too long.
questions.explanation: Leases let ownership recover after control-plane loss.

questions.question: Why is deadline awareness valuable in a scheduler?
options: Some jobs are no longer useful after enough delay and should be dropped or deprioritized.
It removes the need for timestamps.
It guarantees worker pools stay idle.
It only matters for monthly batch jobs.
questions.explanation: Late work can be wasted work.

questions.question: What design change best protects a shared scheduler from one noisy tenant?
options: Per-tenant quotas and priority-aware isolation.
A single retry queue for every tenant combined.
Turning off dead-letter handling.
Removing missed-run tracking.
questions.explanation: Isolation is the platform answer to noisy-neighbor behavior.
